name: Lonnie Johnson
description: American blues and jazz singer, guitarist, violinist and songwriter (1899–1970)
abstract: Alonzo "Lonnie" Johnson (February 8, 1899 – June 16, 1970) was an American blues and jazz singer, guitarist, violinist and songwriter. He was a pioneer of jazz guitar and jazz violin and is recognized as the first to play an electrically amplified violin.

concerts.label: Kid Ory and His Creole Jazz Band

about_quotes.speaker: John Abercrombie
about_quotes.evidence: I don’t know his playing that well... I know that with the duets with Lonnie Johnson it was really interesting... So I thought it was interesting that they were a team that played together.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Abercrombie interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 19, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: Lonnie Johnson, and he lived...he lived in a good place called La Belle, Missouri

about_quotes.speaker: Ernest "Punch" Miller
about_quotes.evidence: Well me and him play together with guitar and trumpet up and down Bollyfoot long time ago.

about_quotes.speaker: Captain Verne Streckfus
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] The Press made one record where it's guitar player Lonnie Johnson played violin. ... [INTERVIEWEE] I don't think they were outstanding to an extent that he just trained them and they were good for him, but they weren't good for anybody else.

about_quotes.speaker: Will Shade and Dewey Corley
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWEE] Lonnie Johnson. ... Sure. In those days, because nowadays he got electric, you see, when he learned to play the guitar, he just playing straight out muscle guitar, making sound like electric guitar dude now.
about_quotes.doc_title: Will Shade and Dewey Corley Interview, 1960-05-25

about_quotes.speaker: Walter "Furry" Lewis
about_quotes.evidence: He used to imitate Lonnie Johnson. He came along. He'd come up with me. Me and him were raised together. ... But he used to copy all of Lonnie Johnson's red blues and play him on the calendar.
